  "analysis": "The training data reveals a consistent pattern for German legal texts: Person entities appear in two main contexts. 1) As the subject of a complaint ('Beschwerdesache [Name]') or 'Beschwerde der/des [Name]'. 2) As the judge/official ('durch den Richter/die Richterin [Title] [Name]'). {
  "analysis": "The training data reveals a consistent pattern for German legal documents: Person entities are almost exclusively preceded by specific titles (e.g., 'Mag.', 'Dr.', 'Prof.', 'Richter', 'Frau', 'Herr') or appear in specific syntactic positions (e.g., 'in der Beschwerdesache [Name]'). The entities often include suffixes like degrees ('Bakk. art.', 'MSc') or professional designations ('ÖkR', 'StR'). The main failure mode in generic NER is missing these titles or including the address that follows the name. The rules must prioritize the title+name structure and stop before the address or comma that typically follows.",
  "strategy": "I will create a set of regex rules that target specific title prefixes common in German legal texts. Each rule will use a capture group to isolate the name (including any trailing degrees) while ensuring the match stops before common delimiters like commas, addresses, or prepositions like 'von'. I will use a high priority for specific title patterns (Mag., Dr., Prof.) and a slightly lower priority for generic honorifics (Frau, Herr) to ensure precision.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "academic_and_professional_titles",
      "description": "Matches names preceded by academic titles (Dr., Prof., Mag., Univ.-Prof., Priv.-Doz., Hon.-Prof.) and professional designations (ÖkR, StR, RgR, KommR, MedR, VetR). Handles gendered suffixes like 'in' or 'a'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:Dr\\.|Dr\\.in|Mag\\.|Mag\\.a|Univ\\.-Prof\\.|Univ\\.-Prof\\.in|Priv\\.-Doz\\.|Priv\\.-Doz\\.in|Hon\\.-Prof\\.|Hon\\.-Prof\\.in|ÖkR|StR|RgR|KommR|MedR|VetR|PhD|MSc|Bakk\\. art\\. MA|Bakk\\. rer\\. nat\\. MBA)\\s+([A-Z][a-zäöüß]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöüß]+)+)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "person"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 10,
      "reasoning": "This is the most common pattern in the training data. It captures the full name including degrees but excludes the title itself. The pattern handles German umlauts and specific degree abbreviations found in the examples."
    },
    {
      "name": "generic_honorifics",
      "description": "Matches names preceded by generic honorifics 'Frau' or 'Herr' which are common in legal descriptions of parties.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:Frau|Herr)\\s+([A-Z][a-zäöüß]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöüß]+)+)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "person"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 8,
      "reasoning": "Covers cases like 'Frau Jennifer K\u00fcfner' or 'Herr \u00d6kR Samir Foellmer' where the title is a generic honorific rather than a specific academic/professional one."
    },
    {
      "name": "role_based_identification",
      "description": "Matches names following role indicators like 'Beschwerdef\u00fchrer', 'Beschwerdef\u00fchrerin', 'Beschuldigter', 'Schriftf\u00fchrer', 'Liquidator', 'Masseverwalter'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:Beschwerdef\u00fchrer|Beschwerdef\u00fchrerin|Beschuldigter|Schriftf\u00fchrer|Liquidator|Masseverwalter)(?:in)?(?:,|=|\\s+)([A-Z][a-zäöüß]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöüß]+)+)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "person"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 7,
      "reasoning": "In legal texts, parties are often introduced by their role. This rule captures the name immediately following the role definition."
    },
    {
      "name": "post_position_names",
      "description": "Matches names appearing after prepositions like 'von', 'des', 'der' in contexts like 'von der Firma X' or 'des X Y' where X Y is a person.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:von|des|der)\\s+([A-Z][a-zäöüß]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöüß]+)+)(?:,|\\s|$)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "person"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Covers cases where the name appears after a preposition, often in phrases like 'von der Firma' (if the firm is named after a person) or 'des X Y'. This is less specific but catches edge cases."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}
